summary: Matt Rocklin argues that open source maintainers should distinguish between critical review comments that prevent bugs and nice-to-have suggestions that add scope. When reviewing PRs, adding enhancement requests to contributors' simple fixes can discourage future contributions and waste everyone's time. He advocates for marking suggestions as optional and recognizing that in professional contexts, review back-and-forth has real costs. The solution is to let good-enough improvements merge quickly, then add enhancements yourself later if they're truly important.
key_points: Distinguish between obligatory comments (preventing regressions) and commendable ones (potential enhancements) in PR reviews
If an enhancement isn't important enough for you to do yourself, you shouldn't require someone else to do it
Professional open source development requires different thinking than volunteer contributions—time has concrete costs
Scope creep in PR reviews is a common problem that undermines the focus professional software engineering requires
Fast PR merges feel great and encourage future contributions; we should optimize for them while keeping code safe
money_quotes: Don't forget that better is the enemy of good, especially when prioritizing against all of the other work that can get done.
This is a good idea! Someone should do this! However, I don't think that it necessarily has to be me.
If they're not something that we're likely to prioritize ourselves, then we definitely shouldn't have been requiring someone else to do this work for us.
The back-and-forth on a PR has very concrete costs (easily rising into the thousands of dollars).
We prioritize with our teams to make sure that we're using our time wisely, but we often forget about this prioritization process when we enter review mode.
These kind folks are well meaning, but they've mistaken your intent as 'clean up the streets' rather than 'get to town'.
Open source is amazing at having strong impact, and at collaboration, but often lacks the focus or direction of professional software engineering shops.
Fast PRs also feel great, we should do more of those, if we can keep them safe.
key_insight: In professional open source, reviewers should mark enhancement suggestions as optional—if you wouldn't prioritize doing it yourself, don't require contributors to do it for you.
